instance.problem_statement:
You run a two-factory manufacturing pipeline. Factory A makes components, then Factory B finishes the same jobs.
Each factory is its own job shop with one-job-per-machine exclusivity and no preemption.
Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 5 jobs, 6 machines in each factory.
Jobs and ordered operations:
- Job 1: Factory A MA2 (1) -> MA5 (7) -> MA1 (7) -> MA6 (1) -> MA4 (8) -> MA3 (5); Factory B MB4 (4) -> MB6 (2) -> MB5 (6) -> MB1 (1) -> MB3 (1) -> MB2 (1)

- Job 3: Factory A MA6 (8) -> MA3 (4) -> MA4 (7) -> MA5 (7) -> MA1 (3) -> MA2 (6); Factory B MB3 (9) -> MB5 (6) -> MB4 (2) -> MB6 (8) -> MB2 (9) -> MB1 (2)

You run a two-factory manufacturing pipeline. Factory A makes components, then Factory B finishes the same jobs.
Each factory is its own job shop with one-job-per-machine exclusivity and no preemption.
Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 5 jobs, 6 machines in each factory.
Jobs and ordered operations:
- Job 1: Factory A MA2 (8) -> MA5 (9) -> MA6 (9) -> MA4 (8) -> MA3 (7) -> MA1 (3); Factory B MB5 (4) -> MB1 (3) -> MB4 (9) -> MB2 (7) -> MB6 (1) -> MB3 (2)
- Job 2: Factory A MA2 (8) -> MA5 (3) -> MA1 (6) -> MA6 (2) -> MA4 (1) -> MA3 (3); Factory B MB4 (8) -> MB5 (4) -> MB6 (5) -> MB3 (7) -> MB2 (5) -> MB1 (7)
- Job 3: Factory A MA5 (9) -> MA4 (2) -> MA3 (4) -> MA6 (5) -> MA2 (5) -> MA1 (2); Factory B MB3 (2) -> MB1 (8) -> MB6 (8) -> MB4 (2) -> MB5 (6) -> MB2 (2)
- Job 4: Factory A MA4 (9) -> MA2 (4) -> MA1 (1) -> MA5 (5) -> MA3 (1) -> MA6 (2); Factory B MB1 (2) -> MB6 (9) -> MB5 (1) -> MB2 (4) -> MB3 (7) -> MB4 (5)
- Job 5: Factory A MA5 (9) -> MA3 (7) -> MA2 (9) -> MA6 (2) -> MA1 (9) -> MA4 (5); Factory B MB3 (7) -> MB6 (4) -> MB2 (5) -> MB4 (7) -> MB5 (5) -> MB1 (9)

Constraints:
- A machine handles at most one job at a time.
- Each job must respect its listed operation order inside each factory.
- Factory B job Jx cannot start until Factory A job Jx has fully completed.
- When you emit a schedule, every required operation must appear exactly once.

A deterministic feasible baseline schedule is provided below. If you stop immediately or fail to produce a valid schedule, this baseline is what gets scored.
Baseline makespan: 170
Baseline schedule summary:
Factory A: MA1: J1[41-44],J2[44-50],J3[70-72],J4[74-75],J5[107-116]; MA2: J1[0-8],J2[8-16],J3[65-70],J4[70-74],J5[96-105]; MA3: J1[34-41],J2[53-56],J3[56-60],J4[80-81],J5[89-96]; MA4: J1[26-34],J2[52-53],J3[53-55],J4[55-64],J5[116-121]; MA5: J1[8-17],J2[17-20],J3[20-29],J4[75-80],J5[80-89]; MA6: J1[17-26],J2[50-52],J3[60-65],J4[81-83],J5[105-107]
Factory B: MB1: J1[48-51],J2[89-96],J3[96-104],J4[104-106],J5[161-170]; MB2: J1[60-67],J2[84-89],J3[120-122],J4[122-126],J5[144-149]; MB3: J1[68-70],J2[77-84],J3[84-86],J4[126-133],J5[133-140]; MB4: J1[51-60],J2[60-68],J3[112-114],J4[133-138],J5[149-156]; MB5: J1[44-48],J2[68-72],J3[114-120],J4[121-122],J5[156-161]; MB6: J1[67-68],J2[72-77],J3[104-112],J4[112-121],J5[140-144]

Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 5 jobs, 6 machines in each factory.
Jobs and ordered operations:
- Job 1: Factory A MA5 (9) -> MA1 (8) -> MA4 (6) -> MA2 (2) -> MA6 (4) -> MA3 (6); Factory B MB4 (1) -> MB6 (7) -> MB3 (3) -> MB5 (6) -> MB1 (7) -> MB2 (7)
- Job 2: Factory A MA3 (1) -> MA6 (4) -> MA4 (3) -> MA1 (4) -> MA2 (5) -> MA5 (9); Factory B MB2 (6) -> MB4 (4) -> MB6 (6) -> MB5 (9) -> MB3 (8) -> MB1 (7)
- Job 3: Factory A MA4 (2) -> MA1 (9) -> MA3 (2) -> MA6 (3) -> MA5 (7) -> MA2 (6); Factory B MB4 (3) -> MB2 (2) -> MB1 (2) -> MB6 (8) -> MB5 (3) -> MB3 (4)
- Job 4: Factory A MA3 (5) -> MA4 (3) -> MA5 (3) -> MA2 (3) -> MA1 (8) -> MA6 (6); Factory B MB2 (6) -> MB5 (7) -> MB6 (4) -> MB1 (1) -> MB4 (9) -> MB3 (1)
- Job 5: Factory A MA3 (9) -> MA6 (5) -> MA2 (9) -> MA1 (8) -> MA4 (8) -> MA5 (2); Factory B MB4 (3) -> MB5 (8) -> MB2 (8) -> MB6 (7) -> MB3 (3) -> MB1 (7)

Constraints:
- A machine handles at most one job at a time.
- Each job must respect its listed operation order inside each factory.
- Factory B job Jx cannot start until Factory A job Jx has fully completed.
- When you emit a schedule, every required operation must appear exactly once.

A deterministic feasible baseline schedule is provided below. If you stop immediately or fail to produce a valid schedule, this baseline is what gets scored.

Factory B: MB1: J1[52-59],J2[99-106],J3[106-108],J4[130-131],J5[169-176]; MB2: J1[59-66],J2[66-72],J3[79-81],J4[91-97],J5[151-159]; MB3: J1[43-46],J2[91-99],J3[119-123],J4[140-141],J5[166-169]; MB4: J1[35-36],J2[72-76],J3[76-79],J4[131-140],J5[140-143]; MB5: J1[46-52],J2[82-91],J3[116-119],J4[119-126],J5[143-151]; MB6: J1[36-43],J2[76-82],J3[108-116],J4[126-130],J5[159-166]

instance.problem_statement:
You run a two-factory manufacturing pipeline. Factory A makes components, then Factory B finishes the same jobs.
Each factory is its own job shop with one-job-per-machine exclusivity and no preemption.
Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 6 jobs, 7 machines in each factory.

sub_instance.problem_statement:
You run a two-factory manufacturing pipeline. Factory A makes components, then Factory B finishes the same jobs.
Each factory is its own job shop with one-job-per-machine exclusivity and no preemption.
Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 5 jobs, 6 machines in each factory.
Jobs and ordered operations:
- Job 1: Factory A MA3 (1) -> MA6 (7) -> MA2 (3) -> MA5 (1) -> MA1 (5) -> MA4 (3); Factory B MB2 (2) -> MB6 (5) -> MB5 (8) -> MB1 (7) -> MB3 (3) -> MB4 (5)

sub_instance.problem_statement:
You run a two-factory manufacturing pipeline. Factory A makes components, then Factory B finishes the same jobs.
Each factory is its own job shop with one-job-per-machine exclusivity and no preemption.
Every job must finish all of its Factory A operations before that same job may start any Factory B operation.
Your objective is to minimize the coupled makespan: the time when the final Factory B operation finishes.

Instance size: 5 jobs, 6 machines in each factory.
Jobs and ordered operations:
- Job 1: Factory A MA6 (1) -> MA1 (1) -> MA5 (2) -> MA3 (4) -> MA2 (4) -> MA4 (9); Factory B MB2 (1) -> MB6 (9) -> MB1 (4) -> MB3 (9) -> MB4 (7) -> MB5 (4)
- Job 2: Factory A MA4 (2) -> MA5 (7) -> MA3 (2) -> MA1 (6) -> MA6 (6) -> MA2 (5); Factory B MB3 (1) -> MB6 (8) -> MB2 (9) -> MB1 (2) -> MB4 (7) -> MB5 (2)
- Job 3: Factory A MA5 (7) -> MA3 (5) -> MA6 (8) -> MA4 (6) -> MA1 (3) -> MA2 (6); Factory B MB1 (6) -> MB2 (4) -> MB3 (5) -> MB6 (2) -> MB4 (3) -> MB5 (9)
- Job 4: Factory A MA6 (4) -> MA2 (1) -> MA5 (6) -> MA4 (7) -> MA3 (5) -> MA1 (2); Factory B MB6 (4) -> MB5 (6) -> MB2 (4) -> MB3 (8) -> MB4 (7) -> MB1 (8)
- Job 5: Factory A MA2 (2) -> MA3 (1) -> MA6 (2) -> MA1 (3) -> MA4 (3) -> MA5 (7); Factory B MB5 (2) -> MB4 (7) -> MB3 (7) -> MB1 (8) -> MB6 (9) -> MB2 (5)

Constraints:
- A machine handles at most one job at a time.
- Each job must respect its listed operation order inside each factory.
- Factory B job Jx cannot start until Factory A job Jx has fully completed.
- When you emit a schedule, every required operation must appear exactly once.

A deterministic feasible baseline schedule is provided below. If you stop immediately or fail to produce a valid schedule, this baseline is what gets scored.

Factory B: MB1: J1[31-35],J2[67-69],J3[69-75],J4[123-131],J5[137-145]; MB2: J1[21-22],J2[58-67],J3[75-79],J4[104-108],J5[154-159]; MB3: J1[35-44],J2[49-50],J3[79-84],J4[108-116],J5[130-137]; MB4: J1[44-51],J2[69-76],J3[86-89],J4[116-123],J5[123-130]; MB5: J1[51-55],J2[76-78],J3[89-98],J4[98-104],J5[104-106]; MB6: J1[22-31],J2[50-58],J3[84-86],J4[88-92],J5[145-154]

instance.problem_statement:
You run a job-shop with 8 jobs across 8 machines (M1..M8).
Each job is an ordered sequence of operations. Machines are exclusive (at most one operation per machine at a time) and operations cannot be preempted. Each job must respect its listed operation order.

Objective:
- makespan = the completion time of the last job
- weighted_tardiness = sum over jobs of tardiness_weight * max(0, completion_time - due_date)
- objective = 20 * makespan + weighted_tardiness
- lower is better

Instance data:
- Job J1: M7 (4) -> M1 (8) -> M3 (14) -> M2 (14) -> M4 (12) -> M8 (12); due_date=237; tardiness_weight=3
- Job J2: M4 (36) -> M6 (24) -> M7 (15) -> M1 (48) -> M3 (36) -> M2 (45); due_date=363; tardiness_weight=2

- Job J5: M2 (27) -> M3 (45) -> M6 (39) -> M1 (33) -> M7 (15) -> M5 (24); due_date=334; tardiness_weight=3
- Job J6: M8 (10) -> M2 (11) -> M1 (11) -> M7 (8) -> M6 (12) -> M3 (14); due_date=123; tardiness_weight=9
- Job J7: M1 (12) -> M2 (13) -> M7 (14) -> M3 (10) -> M5 (10) -> M8 (7); due_date=230; tardiness_weight=3
- Job J8: M7 (48) -> M8 (30) -> M1 (42) -> M6 (24) -> M2 (33) -> M3 (27); due_date=385; tardiness_weight=1

Constraints:
- Each machine handles at most one operation at a time.
- Each listed operation must appear exactly once in your schedule.
- Start/end times must be non-negative integers and end - start must equal the listed operation duration.
- Your BEST_GUESS JSON must contain a `machines` object keyed by machine name (M1..M8), plus `makespan`, `weighted_tardiness`, and `objective`.

sub_instance.problem_statement:
Coastal Emergency scenario:
Storm season is coming. You need to wire a resilient emergency communication network across coastal villages.

You may choose any subset of the available cable links, but the chosen cable network must be a single connected tree that includes every required terminal village.
Every village that lies in your chosen network is active and must be assigned a radio frequency.
If an interference pair is active on both ends, those two villages must use different frequencies.

Objective:
total_cost = sum(cable costs used) + 15 * num_frequencies_used

Villages:
- Port, Bay, Cliff, Pine, Rock, Dune, Mill, Cape

Required terminals:
- Port, Bay, Dune

Available cable links:
- Bay-Cliff: cost 8
- Bay-Pine: cost 8
- Bay-Rock: cost 20
- Bay-Mill: cost 4
- Bay-Cape: cost 9
- Cliff-Pine: cost 10

- Pine-Port: cost 12

- Dune-Port: cost 7

- Mill-Pine: cost 8
- Cape-Port: cost 19
- Cape-Cliff: cost 2
- Cape-Pine: cost 9
- Cape-Rock: cost 11
- Cape-Dune: cost 21
- Cape-Mill: cost 12

Interference pairs:
- Bay / Cliff
- Bay / Mill
- Cliff / Rock
- Cliff / Dune
- Cliff / Mill
- Pine / Port
- Dune / Port
- Dune / Pine

Rules:
- Your cable choice must be a tree, not a cyclic network.
- All required terminals must lie in one connected component.
- Frequencies matter only for active villages in the chosen network.
- If you stop immediately or fail to produce a valid answer, the baseline below is what gets scored.
